title: Competing-risk next-lap hazard: mandatory-rule stop vs performance/tactical stop
summary: FIA rules create a distinct stop motive in dry races: teams may stop because they still owe a second slick compound, separately from stops caused by degradation, undercut defense, or weather.
rationale: FIA rules create a distinct stop motive in dry races: teams may stop because they still owe a second slick compound, separately from stops caused by degradation, undercut defense, or weather. Public survival literature also supports modeling heterogeneous event mechanisms rather than one pooled hazard.
implementation_hint: Train a 2-head discrete-time hazard or mixture-of-experts model: one head for rule/compound-debt pressure and one head for tactical/performance pressure, with a gate driven by wet/dry compound, prior slick count, prior pit count, race progress, and late-race context. Final `PitNextLap` is the summed hazard. Use only out-of-fold pseudo-regime labels if you derive them from target-linked transitions.
expected_effect: Should reduce label-noise from pooling qualitatively different pit decisions into one classifier and improve late-race dry-race ranking where rule debt matters.
risk: If the regime split is badly specified, this collapses back to the existing stop-debt features with extra variance. Keep the gate simple and cross-fitted.
